labels.name: duplicate
labels.description: This issue or pull request already exists

labels.name: wontfix
labels.description: This will not be worked on

issues.title: NIT: Replace NULL with NUL
issues.author: xorrkaz
issues.body: In Section 2.1, the text refers to padding Object Payload with NULL characters if it does not end on a 32-bit boundary.  I think this should read "NUL bytes" or "NUL characters" instead.

issues.title: Adjust interface name and index references
issues.author: xorrkaz
issues.body:
This document refers to the ietf-interfaces.yang module when making reference to an interface's name and index.  Section 2.1 states:

>If the Interface Identification Object identifies the probed interface by index, the length is equal to 8 and the payload contains the if-index [[RFC8343](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8343)].

However, in other parts of the document, the older MIB names of ifName and ifIndex are used.  It would be clearer if the referenced YANG module names were used throughout.

That is, in Section 4, replace ifName with name and ifIndex with if-index (these match the referred RFC).  And in Section 7 (if it stays in the final document), replace ifName with name.

issues.title: Limit the address families used
issues.author: xorrkaz
issues.body:
This document describes that when the L bit is not set, one can specify an interface address using:

>All values found in the IANA registry of Address Family Numbers (available from [[IANA.address-family-numbers](http://www.iana.org/assignments/address-family-numbers)]) are valid in this field.

However, in Section 1, it is mentioned that resolution for a non-local address will be done using either ARP or NDP table lookups.  While IPv4, IPv6, and MAC can be found in these tables, other AFIs may not be.  As such, I think the text should be limited to AFIs for IPv4, IPv6, and 48-bit MAC alone.  Any other AFI would result in a malformed message as the lookup cannot be performed.  

Alternatively, if other lookups could be supported, that could be described here or simply left for other extension documents that might update this document.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body:
Thanks for bringing this up, Joe.  [Section 4.1](https://fenner.github.io/probe-clarification/draft-fenner-intarea-probe-clarification.html#name-code-field-processing) says:

> The Code field MUST be set to (3) No Such Table Entry if the L-bit
> is clear and the address found in the Interface Identification Object
> does not appear in the IPv4 Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) table or
> the IPv6 Neighbor Cache.

In my mind, this is sufficient - if you set the AF to IPX, and that IPX address doesn't appear in the IPv4 ARP or IPv6 neighbor cache (hint: it can't), then you return No Such Table Entry - so we have a well defined behavior in the "arbitrary AF" case.

If we try to go down the path of limiting this in the address field specification, we end up having to differentiate based on the setting of the L bit - since it's actually valid to ask about the interface with IPX address foo if L=1.

comments.author: xorrkaz
comments.body: I think the "No Such Table Entry" error is fine in this case.

issues.title: Update IANA Considerations
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
IANA's pre-meeting review for IETF120 included:

The actions described in the IANA Considerations section were completed for RFC 8335. Should IANA replace references to RFC 8355 with references to this document? If so, please add a sentence to the beginning of the section that instructs us to do so. If not, please update the section to note that no IANA actions are required (and move the 8335 actions to another section, if possible).

issues.title: Leave the door open to a different packet format if a different extension object is present
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
From Ron:
> Currently, RFC 8335 says that the Extended Echo Request MUST contain exactly one instance of the Interface Identifier Object.  I would like to replace that text with the following:

> "The Extended Echo Request message MUST contain zero or one instances of the Interface Identification Object. If the Interface Identification Object is absent, the probed interface is identified by Destination Address in the IP header".

> This change will allow us to reuse the Extended Echo Request and Extended Echo Reply message in another application (Loopback 6)

comments.author: fenner
comments.body:
My proposal to be a little less proscriptive, something like

> “Extended Echo contains an ICMP Extension structure that defines the operation to perform. When it contains the Echo Request, it MUST … The behavior when it contains a different extension object is not defined by this memo but may be defined in the future”

issues.title: WG Last Call comment from Nick Buraglio
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/int-area/q-UIncPVXI93WB325xn5OBX-2tE/

Good feedback on wording

1.

> I know what you're going for here, but I found this a bit hard to digest. I had to read it a few times.
> `The probed interface can reside on or directly connect to the proxy node.`
> Maybe
> `The probed interface can reside on or be directly connected to the proxy node.`

2.

> Section 9.
> Suggest extending the section
> ```
> PROBE must not leak information about one Virtual Private Network
> (VPN) into another.  Therefore, when a node receives an ICMP Extended
> Echo Request and the proxy interface is in a different VPN than the
> probed interface, the node MUST return an ICMP Extended Echo Reply
> with error code equal to (2) No Such Interface.
>    
> ```
> to include virtual systems / vsys / logical systems and perhaps containerization where a given software based, logical separation may require different security postures.
> Perhaps
> ```
> PROBE must not leak information about one Virtual Private Network (VPN), logical system, or other software partitioned interface or set of interfaces
> into another.  Therefore, when a node receives an ICMP Extended
> Echo Request and the proxy interface is in a different VPN than the
> probed interface, the node MUST return an ICMP Extended Echo Reply
> with error code equal to (2) No Such Interface.
>    
> ```
>

issues.title: Shepherd issue: address WG Last Call points
issues.author: fenner
issues.body: - Please make sure to address and reply to the comments sent in WG last call.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: All issues brought up in last call have been handled, by document updates or discussion on the mailing list.

issues.title: Shepherd issue: MTU
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
- The document does not discuss the MTU, and whether the optional data
can be large enough to cause the packet to exceed the MTU. It would be
best to specify that the Extended Echo Request/Reply packets SHOULD
NOT exceed the MTU.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: In fact, we can say that the reply packets MUST NOT exceed the MTU, since the v4 packet is formed with DF set already. I'd be fine with saying request packets MUST NOT exceed the MTU too.

issues.title: Shepherd issue: Security considerations
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
- The security considerations should be extended to include missing
aspects such as amplification, covert channels, malicious
modifications. Please see the Security Considerations section of
draft-ietf-6man-icmpv6-reflection, which was significantly affected by
the Security AD reviews.

issues.title: Shepherd issue: add circular reference to icmp-reflection
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
- Please note that this document and draft-ietf-6man-icmpv6-reflection
will most probably be published as a cluster. It would be very helpful
for the readers to have an explicit reference to the reflection draft,
specifically the sentence "The behavior when it contains a different
Extension Object is not defined by this memo but may be defined in the
future." should be rephrased to mention
draft-ietf-6man-icmpv6-reflection as an example of such a case.

issues.title: Tony Przygienda: L-bit, proxy node wording
issues.author: fenner
issues.body: The “L-bit” and the whole “directly connected” vs. “resides on proxy” is baffling for a long time in the doc and looks to me like a distinction w/o difference. I suggest maybe a better terminology and definitely a clear glossary with an earlier explanation of the terms. It’s confusing to call it  a “proxy node” while the interface is still on the “proxy node”. Maybe the “local” bit should be called “proxy bit” or something to read it clearer.

comments.author: rejithomas-arista
comments.body: I see the L-bit naming was discussed on the mailing list, and since Tony did not press further once the rationale for keeping the existing "Local" terminology was explained, this can considered resolved and closed?.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: Close as Reji suggests.

issues.title: Tony Przygienda: can not distinguish same link-local address on different interfaces
issues.author: fenner
issues.body: Draft may have a roblem if the node has multiple interfaces with LLv6 only that are the same (which is allowed). Then if the L-bit is clear you cannot differentiate given the “MUST identify by address” since you need interface name as well in this case. AFAIS a clarification is needed that you can use both address and name with L bit clear than then have procedures to resolve ambiguity (e.g. interface name always takes precedence if address mismatches) . AFAIS the interface identification object however is allowed only once.

issues.title: Tony Przygienda: clarification required for "resides on" / "directly connected"
issues.author: fenner
issues.body: Section 5 claims to clarify the language for “resides on” and “directly connected” but that does never really happen in the draft AFAIS.

issues.title: Tony Przygienda: "yang proxy"
issues.author: fenner
issues.body: Generally I'm bit terrified of the whole thing acting as a “yang proxy”. The probing node could simply read the according Yang and if it cannot then the whole “proxy” is basically bypassing Yang security restrictions AFAIS? and yes, I understand, it's all "exigency" and "e'one already does it" but then the draft should be maybe bit more honest about it and deal with according security implications ?

comments.author: rejithomas-arista
comments.body: The "YANG proxy" characterization was discussed on the mailing list — YANG is a modeling language, not a protocol, and PROBE is disabled by default requiring explicit admin enablement. Since Tony did not press further, this I believe can be considered resolved.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: Close as Reji suggests.

issues.title: Early intdir review
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
The document is generally fine, with the following offered as a suggestion for clarity:

Page 10 should be updated with the text in braces []:

Otherwise, when a node receives an ICMPv4 Extended Echo Request, it
   MUST format [the IPv4 header of] an ICMP Extended Echo Reply as follows:
   *  Don't Fragment (DF) flag is 1
   *  More Fragments flag is 0
   *  Fragment Offset is 0
  [(the above indicate that the packet is not source fragmented and must not be on-path fragmented)]
   *  TTL is 255
   *  Protocol is ICMP
   When a node receives an ICMPv6 Extended Echo Request, it MUST format
   [the IPv6 header of] an ICMPv6 Extended Echo Reply as follows:
   *  Hop Limit is 255
   *  Next Header is ICMPv6
  [* No use of the IPv6 Fragmentation Header (i.e., not source fragmented)]

issues.title: Opsdir review: the document lacks a management considerations sectino
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
it lacks a management/operations considerations section

A complete set of _"Guidelines for Considering Operations and Management in
IETF Specifications"_ can be found at
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-opsawg-rfc5706bis/.

The management/operations section should provide tips for managing pings/probes
within a network to prevent overloading routers or hosts.

issues.title: Opsdir review: the document lacks a security considerations section
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
it lacks ... a security considerations section

The Security section
should talk about the critical information found in interface names,
identifiers or addresses. This advice is not in RFC4884 (which this updates).

comments.author: fenner
comments.body:
The document already talked about names:

>    However, malicious parties can use PROBE to obtain additional
>    information.  For example, a malicious party can use PROBE to
>    discover interface names.  Having discovered an interface name, the
>    malicious party may be able to infer additional information.
>    Additional information may include:
> 
>    *  interface bandwidth
> 
>    *  the type of device that supports the interface (e.g., vendor
>       identity)
> 
>    *  the operating system version that the above-mentioned device
>       executes
> 

I added this paragraph to additionally talk about addresses and interface indexes:

>    Addresses and interface index values can also give away information
>    that might not want to be shared.  For example, a malicious party can
>    use PROBE to determine that a given IP address is assigned to any
>    interface on the probed node, or if interface index values are
>    assigned densely, it can determine how many interfaces exist on the
>    probed node.
>

issues.title: Opsdir nit: section 1.3
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
Section 1.3 needs to untangle the English in this sentence to multiple
sentences.

Old text:/
>   This document defines a unique use of ICMP Extensions [RFC4884]:
>   while normally, ICMP Extensions are defined to start at a given point
>   and continue to the end of the packet, if the extension object is an
>   Interface Identification Object as defined in this memo, then the
>   extension structure (including the checksum) consists only of that
>   single ICMP Extension Object. /

It can be written as 2 sentence to clarify.

issues.title: Opsdir nit: don't do this at all
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
RFC8335 behavior being extended.

Sometimes, it is just necessary to code around the bad behavior of initial
implementations. However, the authors should realize that the IESG really,
really hates a document that codes around the bad behavior of an RFC that is
being deprecated.

Just thought you should think about this carefully.  The bad behavior also
allows some data to be passed after the Interface Identification Object
without having any checksum or integrity. Seems like a questionable
concept.  I was looking for the security section to explain to me why
you thought this was reasonable

issues.title: Opsdir nit: don't talk about future extension
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
Editorial NIT section 2.0, paragraph starting with Section 7. last sentence

old text/ 
>  The behavior when it contains a different Extension
>    Object is not defined by this memo but may be defined in the future./

New text:/
>   The behavior when it contains a different Extension
>    Object is not defined by this memo./

We are the IETF, new standards can always update old standards.
The last clause for this sentence is not needed.

comments.author: rejithomas-arista
comments.body: The "may be defined in the future" language was discussed on the mailing list and there is value in keeping explicit forward-looking statements. Can be considered as resolved.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: Close as Reji suggests.

issues.title: Opsdir review: Fault Management
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
Fault Management: Are failure detection/recovery mechanisms specified?
Error handling for packet fields is covered in ICMP Message Processing
Code Field processing. It would be good to know what happens, if experience
(which is evident by the bis), has denoted any other failure conditions.

issues.title: Opsdir review: Configuration Management
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
Configuration Management: Are configuration changes to enable/disable the
feature clearly defined? The configuration is hinted at in main document and
Appendix A (with application). =It would be useful to know if a YANG model is
planned/exists.

issues.title: Opsdir review: Performance Monitoring
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
Performance Monitoring: Are metrics (e.g., latency, resource usage) clearly
identified? I did not find hte performance metics for correct operations of the
code.

issues.title: Opsdir review: deployment
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
As a BIS, this document must have hints for deployment and
managed in a network.  That wisdom | |                  | is missing (probably
should go to manageability/operations section)                           |

issues.title: Opsdir review: Migration path
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
The RFC8335 path plan is to allow "stuff" after ICMP
Extension Structure. When "stuff" is     | |                  | allowed after
valid fields without an integrity checksum, bad things can be passed

issues.title: Opsdir review: transition from
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
It would be good to know if early deployments have found
any problems when transition from    | | RFC8335 to bis   | RFC8335 to this
solution.  Does this solution help ward off DOS attacks? Is there a place     |
|                  | during the transition that might cause operational issues.
                                   |

issues.title: Opsdir review: Impact on network
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
What happens when this protocol gets used to attack a
node?  Is there more traffic on node    | | Operation control|  when this
happens: |  plane           |                                                 
                                            |

issues.title: Opsdir review: Verifying Correct
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
Has a test plan been written for performance for this new
feature?  if so, can it be shared? | | Operation        | Do utilies show up
ETE probe timings like Ping timing? What load on routers or hosts?        |

issues.title: Last Call comment from Xiao Min
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
I have a few comments on draft-ietf-intarea-rfc8335bis for the authors to consider.
Section 2, it says "
>   The Interface Identification Object MAY be followed by an optional
>   data section, which is not interpreted but is simply present to be
>   copied to the ICMP Extended Echo Reply.

However it lacks an explanation on why *an optional data section* is added on the basis of RFC 8335.
Section 3, similarly it lacks an explanation on why the *ICMP Extension Structure* and "Data" fields are added on the basis of RFC 8335. Furthermore, it seems helpful to the reader if the definition/processing of the two newly added fields can be put in the place closer to the definition/processing of other fields in the ICMP Extended Echo Reply message. Currently the only text on the two newly added fields is placed in the next section (Section 4).

comments.author: rejithomas-arista
comments.body: Xiao Min withdrew the concern on-list Feb 3: "please feel free to ignore them."

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: Close as Reji suggests.

issues.title: Confirmation of the SIX authors
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

As there are 6 authors, i.e., larger than the usual limit of 5, I wonder whether the editor (who apparently did most of the work in the -bis draft) and the other authors have considered moving the RFC 8335 authors (who did not provide text to this -bis) to the Contributors section.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: I haven't had this discussion; I was hoping for guidance like this because I didn't want to sound like I wanted to minimize the other authors' contributions.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body:
Just some notes, maybe incomplete:
- Chris contributed the section about human-readable formatting
- Reji contributed to the security & management considerations sections

issues.title: Sections 2 and 2.1
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

When the probed interface is identified by address, please be clear that this is a unicast address or can it also be multicast, any cast, or broadcast?

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: Yes, it must be unicast.

issues.title: Section 4.1
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

Suggest to extend the description of `No Such Table Entry` case when the Interface Identification Object is not an IPv[46] address, e.g., a data-link layer address as indicated by the AFI being neither 1 nor 2.

It is also unclear whether the state of the NDP/ARP cache entry is important, I guess that an cache entry in the 'unreachable' state should return 'not ok', but I was unable to find where it is specified in the text.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body:
On AFI being neither 1 nor 2, sure, I can do that.

On the topic of the state, I suppose "unreachable" being missing was an oversight. It makes sense to me.

I couldn't find any mention of state in RFC 826; I imagine that different OSes might have different implementations of state in this case. Do you have a good reference that I can point to to allow implementors to understand what to report when?

RFC 4861 defines the states to be INCOMPLETE, REACHABLE, STALE, DELAY, and PROBE. I guess "unreachable" includes INCOMPLETE, DELAY and PROBE? Obviously REACHABLE is not unreachable, and STALE kind of says "not not reachable... yet".?

issues.title: Interface enumeration
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

Should there be a security consideration point about enumerating the interfaces as the if-index values can probably and quickly be enumerated ?

comments.author: fenner
comments.body:
Addresses and interface index values are considered together in this paragraph.  I've replaced the address considerations with ... to make the interface index considerations more clear. Does this address your issue?

... interface index values can also give away information
that might not want to be shared. For example, a malicious party can
use PROBE to ... if interface index values are
assigned densely, it can determine how many interfaces exist on the
probed node.

issues.title: Dual-stack behaviour
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

As I hope that sending an ICMPv4 Echo Extended Request to a router, asking about an interface that has only an IPv6 address is allowed, should there be text about this nice feature ?

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: Sure, I thought it was obvious, but I can add text.

issues.title: Abstract
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

s/This document describes a network diagnostic /This document _specifies_ a network diagnostic / ? as it is a proposed standard

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: Sure.

issues.title: Section 4
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

What about the IPv6 flow label ?

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: I am not creative enough to come up with a use case for the flow label in this protocol. It's most obvious to say MBZ. Is that reasonable to you?

issues.title: Section 7
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

It is unclear whether the change history needs to be kept (there is no RFC Editor note), I would prefer to keep the diff with RFC 8335 while deleting the history of the drafts.

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: I think I'll add another section with the diffs from RFC8335, and mark the current change log as to be removed.

issues.title: Acknowledgments
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

Should the acknowlegments for RFC 8335 be removed or explicitely marked "for RFC 8335" ?

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: I don't think so, but I'm happy to accept guidance here. Most of RFC 8335 is still there, so it makes sense to me to keep the acknowledgements of the people who made that happen.

comments.author: evyncke
comments.body: Up to you

issues.title: Return of deployment experience with RFC 8335
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

A small section about the experience gained with RFC 8335 (dated 2018) would be useful, possibly in the appendix. E.g., whether the PROBE packets were able to be sent over the public Internet and/or through middleboxes (for some definitions of middleboxes ;-) ).

comments.author: fenner
comments.body: I was able to send PROBE across the public Internet when I was testing out implementations a couple of years ago. I am not sure how to test with middleboxes.

comments.author: evyncke
comments.body: What about the co-authors return of experience ? If there is none, then it is somehow annoying to spend time on this draft :-(

issues.title: Use of SVG graphics
issues.author: fenner
issues.body:
_Comment by @evyncke_

To make a much nicer HTML rendering, suggest using the aasvg too to generate SVG graphics. It is worth a try especially as this I-D uses the Kramdown file format by starting the block with `~~~ aasvg` ;-)

pulls.title: Add Manageability Considerations section
pulls.author: rejithomas-arista
pulls.body:
Add new Section 10 (Manageability Considerations) immediately before Security Considerations, per RFC 5706 Section 4.3 guidance.

Subsections cover:
- Control of Function and Policy (configuration parameters)
- Monitoring and Verifying Operation (verification, counters, RTT)
- Deployment and Backward Compatibility (guidance, RFC 8335 compat)
- Impact on Network Operation (resource cost, rate-limiting)

Fixes #16, fixes #21, fixes #22, fixes #23, fixes #24, fixes #26, fixes #28

pulls.title: Expand Security Considerations
pulls.author: rejithomas-arista
pulls.body:
Add paragraphs addressing:
- Amplification risk 
- Covert channel potential
- On-path attacker modification 
- ICMP header checksum scope 

Fixes #9, fixes #27

pulls.title: Address WG Last Call comments from Nick Buraglio
pulls.author: rejithomas-arista
pulls.body:
- Fix grammar: "directly connect to" -> "be directly connected to"
- Broaden VPN isolation language to cover network instances (RFC 8529) and logical network elements (RFC 8530), not just VPNs

Fixes #6

pulls.title: Improve readability of Section 1.3 (ICMP Extensions note)
pulls.author: rejithomas-arista
pulls.body:
Split the opening sentence into shorter, clearer sentences per OPSDIR review feedback. No semantic change.

Fixes #18

pulls.title: Clarify ICMP Extension Structure checksum scope in Section 1.3
pulls.author: rejithomas-arista
pulls.body:
Add text explaining that the Extension Structure checksum covers only the Interface Identification Object, while any data following it is protected by the ICMP header checksum which covers the entire message. Cross-references Security Considerations for further discussion.

Fixes #19, fixes #25
